cveMetadata.datePublished: EMBARGOED — publish only after fix confirmed deployed

cna.title: Hearth: PKCE not enforced for authorization code flow — authorization code injection

descriptions.value: Hearth, an OIDC/OAuth 2.0 identity provider, does not enforce Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE) on the authorization code flow. The `code_challenge` and `code_challenge_method` parameters in authorization requests are treated as optional for all client types, including public clients. An attacker who obtains a victim's authorization code — through redirect URI interception, referrer leakage, network eavesdropping, or a compromised intermediary — can exchange it at the token endpoint without supplying a `code_verifier`, bypassing the binding that PKCE is designed to enforce. This constitutes an authorization code injection attack as described in RFC 9700 §4.5 and draft-ietf-oauth-security-topics.

supportingMedia.value: Hearth does not enforce PKCE on the authorization code flow. The code_challenge and code_challenge_method parameters are optional for all client types, including public clients. An attacker who obtains an authorization code can exchange it at the token endpoint without a code_verifier, bypassing PKCE's code-binding protection.

descriptions.description: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function — the token endpoint does not require a PKCE code verifier, meaning the proof-of-possession step is absent for clients that omit PKCE in the authorization request.
CWE-287: Improper Authentication — public clients can complete an authorization code exchange without demonstrating they initiated the authorization request.

scenarios.value: Attacker intercepts or acquires an authorization code and exchanges it without a code_verifier.

references.name: RFC 9700 — OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice
RFC 7636 — Proof Key for Code Exchange by OAuth Public Clients
FAPI 2.0 Security Profile — mandates PKCE S256 for all clients

timeline.value: Finding identified via internal security audit (SecurityResearcher agent, HEA-481).
Embargo lifted and advisory published after fix confirmed deployed.

credits.value: Hearth internal security audit — SecurityResearcher agent

workarounds.value: As an interim measure, configure all client applications to always send PKCE S256 parameters in authorization requests. Clients that omit `code_challenge` will still be accepted by the server until the fix is deployed, but enforcing PKCE client-side reduces the attack window. Only register clients over `https://` redirect URIs. Avoid use of the implicit flow.

supportingMedia.value: Enforce PKCE S256 client-side in all OAuth clients. Avoid implicit flow and HTTP redirect URIs as mitigating posture until server-side enforcement is deployed.

x_internalNotes.embargoLifts: After fix confirmed deployed — NOT solely on merge of HEA-501.
x_internalNotes.ctoApprovalStatus: APPROVED — 2026-05-15 (CTO: brad@inteletech.io). Correction applied: A:H→A:N (PKCE bypass has no availability impact; C:H/I:H covers account takeover). Score revised 8.1→7.4. Code evidence line numbers reflect pre-fix snapshot; fix is now on feature/more-gap-fixes pending merge.

codeEvidence.description: Pre-fix state: PKCE fields are Option<T>; engine only validates PKCE when code_challenge is Some. NOTE: Fix is now in feature/more-gap-fixes — line numbers reflect pre-merge snapshot and have shifted. Verify against git history (commit before 'More gap fixes' on feature/more-gap-fixes) for exact pre-fix evidence.
files: src/identity/oidc.rs — code_challenge: Option<String>, code_challenge_method: Option<CodeChallengeMethod> (still Option in fixed code; enforcement added at authorization layer)
src/identity/engine.rs (pre-fix) — no mandatory PKCE check on public clients at authorize step
src/identity/engine.rs (pre-fix) — token exchange PKCE check conditional on stored_code.code_challenge being Some, skipped entirely when absent
src/identity/engine.rs (post-fix ~line 4015) — added: if !client.is_confidential() && request.code_challenge.is_none() { return Err(...) } — now enforces PKCE for public clients

x_internalNotes.specReference: RFC 9700 §2.1.1 — 'Authorization servers MUST support PKCE [RFC7636]. ... Authorization servers MUST require the use of PKCE for public clients.' RFC 9700 §4.5 — Authorization Code Injection attack class.
